coredata.dc:title: Neem seed derived green C-dots: A highly sensitive luminescent probe for aqueous Au3+ ions and nurtures green gold recovery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Raji, Kaviyarasan
Thiyagarajan, Senthil Kumar
Suresh, Raghupathy
Vadivel, Ramanan
Palanivel, Dharmalingam
Ramamurthy, Perumal
coredata.dc:description: Highly luminescent green C-dots (NeCDs) were synthesized using neem seed kernel as the carbon precursor via one step microwave assisted pyrolysis. The brown NeCDs powder ensued after purification readily disperses in water (avg. particle size ~ 2 nm) and exhibits extensive surface functionalization of the carbon core by carboxyl and poly-hydroxyl functional groups. The aqueous solution of NeCDs is stable, has long shelf life and exhibit high photostability in different environments. In addition, NeCDs is found to be harmless and nontoxic towards A549 cells, and hence it was employed as a luminescent probe for multicolor cell imaging. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) of NeCDs solution (φf ~ 12.5%) is significantly/selectively quenched in the presence of Au3+ ions via two distinct deactivation processes viz donor excited photo-induced electron transfer and co-ordination induced aggregation. As a result, NeCDs exhibit the lowest ever reported limit of detection (LOD) of ~16 nM for aqueous Au3+ ions. Prolonged exposure (~ 24 h) to the poly-hydroxyl surface group of NeCDs chemically reduces the co-ordinated Au3+ ions at room temperature even in dark and produces urchin-like gold nanoparticles (UL-Au NPs), a simple and greener way to recover gold from aqueous medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neem seed kernel
Nontoxic
Multicolor cell imaging
Selective sensing of Au3+
Recovery of gold
Urchin-like gold nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation and extraction of urchin-like gold nanoparticles (UL-Au NPs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (b) Particle size distribution (c) XRD pattern and (d) Raman spectrum of NeCDs.
FT-IR spectrum of neem seed kernel and NeCDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of NeCDs. High resolution spectra of (b) C 1s and (c) O 1s peaks.

(a) Absorption spectrum of NeCDs. Inset: NeCDs solution at daylight and 365 nm irradiation. (b) Excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra (c) Normalized PL spectra and (d) Emission wavelength dependent excitation spectra of NeCDs.
(a) 2D contour map (b) PL quantum yield and (c) PL lifetime decay profiles at different emission wavelengths of the NeCDs.

(a) PL intensity of NeCDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions, (b) PL intensity of NeCDs in the presence of Au3+ ions at different concentrations from 0 to 25 μM, and (c) Plot of change in the emission intensity (F0-F) of NeCDs against concentration of the Au3+ ions.
(a) Stern-Volmer plot (F0/F vs [Au3+]), and (b) PL Decays of NeCDs in the absence and presence of Au3+ ions monitored at 450 nm (λex: 375 nm).
(a–d) TEM image of aggregated NeCDs in the presence of Au3+ ions.
Surface plasmon absorption of the (Au NPs).
Schematic illustration of aggregation of NeCDs with Au3+ ions.
Schematic illustration of synthesis of NeCDs from neem seed kernels.

head.ce:title: Neem seed derived green C-dots: A highly sensitive luminescent probe for aqueous Au3+ ions and nurtures green gold recovery

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Centre for Ultrafast Processes, University of Madras, Taramani Campus, Chennai 600113, Tamil Nadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Ultrafast Processes, University of Madras, Taramani Campus

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly luminescent green C-dots (NeCDs) were synthesized using neem seed kernel as the carbon precursor via one step microwave assisted pyrolysis. The brown NeCDs powder ensued after purification readily disperses in water (avg. particle size ~ 2 nm) and exhibits extensive surface functionalization of the carbon core by carboxyl and poly-hydroxyl functional groups. The aqueous solution of NeCDs is stable, has long shelf life and exhibit high photostability in different environments. In addition, NeCDs is found to be harmless and nontoxic towards A549 cells, and hence it was employed as a luminescent probe for multicolor cell imaging. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) of NeCDs solution (φf ~ 12.5%) is significantly/selectively quenched in the presence of Au3+ ions via two distinct deactivation processes viz donor excited photo-induced electron transfer and co-ordination induced aggregation. As a result, NeCDs exhibit the lowest ever reported limit of detection (LOD) of ~16 nM for aqueous Au3+ ions. Prolonged exposure (~ 24 h) to the poly-hydroxyl surface group of NeCDs chemically reduces the co-ordinated Au3+ ions at room temperature even in dark and produces urchin-like gold nanoparticles (UL-Au NPs), a simple and greener way to recover gold from aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold is an expensive noble metal, which exists in various oxidation states such as 0, +1, and +3. This precious metal widely used in jewellery, modern electronics, medicine and chemical catalysis due to its unique physicochemical properties, and it also plays an important role in the national economy [1–8]. Moreover, the metallic gold is not significantly toxic to humans, but is potentially toxic to the human in ionic forms. For example Au3+ ions can bind firmly to DNA and several enzymes, resulting in a serious damage to the peripheral nervous system, liver, kidney and it also lead to other health issues such as blood dyscrasias, cutaneous reactions, nephrotoxicity, and bone marrow depression, etc [9,10]. Moreover, the gold in nano form having tremendous attention and potential applications in diverse fields such as photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, drug delivery, and bio-sensing due to their excellent biocompatibility, optical and physicochemical properties [11–16]. The global demand of the gold is rapidly increasing day by day because of their worldwide usage. The recovery and recycling of gold have become particularly important due to its limited reserves. This precious material is extracted from ores with a low abundance of gold as compared with the waste secondary resources [17]. The waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE) has been recognized as a significant source of gold because the gold content of WEEE is estimated to be 80 times higher than that found in global primary gold mining deposits [8]. For the last decade, many methods were used to recover gold from ores and WEEE [8,17–24] To recover gold from the ores or WEEE, the following three steps are indispensable: 1) oxidative leaching of gold from raw ore or WEEE, 2) separation and concentration of gold ions from the leaching solution and 3) reduction of extracted gold ions to zerovalent (Au0) gold materials [17]. Nevertheless, as this methodology usually needs high energy consumption and causes serious environmental pollution because hazardous chemicals and organic solvents are employed during the recovery processes. An increasing number of studies have endeavored to develop significant and new environmentally benign techniques with low energy requirement. However, highly selective sensing and recovery of Au3+ is always having a significant difficulty, because other metal ions such as ferric, aluminium, magnesium, nickel, copper and zinc etc. also coexist with gold ion in the waste streams. Hence, a significant new, sustainable, efficient, cost-effective, facile, rapid, environmentally benign, selective sensing and recovery method is needed immediately. Accordingly, the development of highly efficient, easy and eco-friendly methods to recover gold from these waste streams has attracted enormous attention for the researchers. The carbon dots (CDs) have attracted worldwide attention and have been applied in different fields since its discovery [25–31] owing to its excellent optical and physicochemical properties such as very low toxicity and high biocompatibility, high PL stability in diverse environments, excellent solubility in water and organic polar solvents, chemical inertness and electron donor acceptor properties. In the present work, we employed ‘environmentally benign CDs’ as selective candidates for specific sensing and recovery of Au3+ in aqueous media. The optical and structural properties of neem seed-derived CDs are systematically investigated and the synergic role of NeCDs as selective sensor and green reductant towards Au3+ ions is unveiled.

The neem seeds used in the present study were collected from local place in Tamil Nadu, India. o-phosphoric acid (85%) used in synthesis was purchased from Qualigens. Quinine sulfate (98%), the quantum yield reference, was supplied by Fluka. 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazoyl)−2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Potassium chloride, sodium hydroxide and sulfuric acid were purchased from Qualigens to check the PL stability of NeCDs in the presence of electrolytes and at different pH. Gold (III) chloride hydrate and other metal perchlorates used in the PL sensing studies were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as such. The Cellulose ester dialysis membrane (Spectra/Por, Float-A-Lyzer G2, MWCO 500–1000D) was purchased from Spectrum Labs. All solutions were prepared using millipore water from Aquelix 5 water purification system.

The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) Tecnai G2 20 Twin microscope was used to determine the particle size, morphology, and to record the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of NeCDs and UL-Au NPs. Surface morphology of obtained UL-Au NPs was analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM-Quattro S ThermoFisher Scientific). Surface roughness of the UL-Au NPs was estimated by ASYLUM RESEARCH MFP-3D SPM atomic force microscope through non-contact mode. Raman spectrum was recorded by exciting the sample at 532 nm using Raman 11i system from Nanophoton Corp., Japan. The elemental composition of NeCDs was analyzed by ESCA-14 (Omicron Nanotechnology) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and the IR spectra were recorded using Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a XPERT PRO, PANalytical instrument. The zeta potential measurements were carried out using the nanopartica 100-SZ made by HORIBA Scientific.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with Cary 100 Bio UV–vis double beam spectrophotometer. All PL measurements (emission, 3D, excitation) were carried out using Flouromax-4P spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon Inc). The PL decay profiles were recorded using IBH time correlated single photon counting spectrometer with photomultiplier tube as detector and a 375 nm light emitting diode (LED) as the excitation source. The relative PL QY of NeCDs was determined via slope method by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 366 nm) and the absorbance values of NeCDs at 366 nm with those of the reference quinine sulfate.

To check the PL stability of NeCDs, the PL spectra were recorded by exciting the solutions at 360 nm in the presence of electrolytes at different ionic strength (up to 2 M KCl), at different pH (1–14), by exposing it to UV irradiation (365 nm) and by storing the aqueous solution of NeCDs under ambient conditions for 150 days.

Initially, to probe the influence of various metal ions in affecting the PL of NeCDs, the aqueous solutions of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Au3+ were prepared separately for a concentration of about 5 mM (stock solution). After adding 200 μM of these metal ions, the PL of NeCDs was recorded by exciting the respective solutions at 380 nm.

The sensitivity towards Au3+ ions (Limit of Detection, [LOD]) was determined at room temperature by standard addition method. Different concentrations of Au3+ ions ranging from 0 to 25 μM were added to the aqueous solutions of NeCDs and after 5 min, the corresponding PL quenching responses were recorded by exciting the respective solutions at 380 nm.

The cytotoxicity of the NeCDs was examined using a MTT assay. A549 cells were plated in 96- well plate at a concentration of 5 × 103 cells/well in complete medium and incubated overnight. The cells were treated with different concentrations of NeCDs from 0 to 2 mg mL−1 and further incubated the plate at 37 °C for 48 h. At the end of the incubation, 10 µL of MTT stock solution (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) was added to each well and cells incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. After incubation, the MTT solution was carefully removed from the well and 100 µL of DMSO was added to dissolve the insoluble formazan crystals. The resulting intracellular purple formazan was quantified using a microtiter plate reader at 570 nm. The absorbance values were used to estimate the percentage of cell viability using the following equation:
Viable
cells ( % )
= [
Absorbance
of
treated
cells
Absorbance
of
control
cells
]
×
100

A549 cells were plated in 6-well plate at a concentration of 5 × 106 cells/well in complete medium and incubated overnight. After incubation, a dose of 0.2 mg mL−1 of NeCDs solution were (typically 100 µL) added to the culture plate, which was then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Prior to the fixation of the cells on the slide for investigation with a fluorescence microscope, the overdose of NeCDs were carefully removed by washing 3 times with PBS. The fluorescent images were taken at fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse‐80i, Japan) and the images were recorded at 40× magnification with different band pass filters.

Neem seeds used in the present study were collected and dried in sunlight. The seeds were pounded gently to decorticate the hull and it was rejected. It is only the remaining clean neem kernels were used as carbon precursors for synthesizing the CDs. 1 g of this neem kernels were crushed gently (so that no oil comes out) using mortar and pestle and mixed with 10 mL of 40% aqueous solution of o-phosphoric acid (v/v) in a 250 mL beaker. o-phosphoric acid is a weak acid and it was taken not only to passivate the surface through oxidation but also to increase the viscosity of the medium so that it can absorb more microwaves comparatively with water. It is also noteworthy that the dipole moment of phosphoric acid is much higher than water so that it can absorb the MW irradiation effectively than water. The mixture was then kept in a domestic microwave oven (SAMSUNG, Model: MW73BD) at 600 W for 100 s. Emergence of concentrated yellow solution ascertains the formation of CDs and it was allowed to cool at room temperature. The concentrated yellow solution was then diluted with 20 mL of ethanol and drops of 2 N NaOH solution were added drop wise. The unreacted phosphate emerges out as insoluble trisodium phosphate (Na3PO4) and it was filtered through a Whatman 40 grade filter paper. The resultant clear yellow filtrate (pH ~7) was transferred into a polycarbonate centrifuge tube and subjected to centrifugation (REMI) at 15,000 rpm for 15 min. The clear supernatant solution was then transferred into a pre-treated, cellulose ester dialysis membrane of 10 mL capacity. Dialysis was carried out for 48 h to remove any small molecules or products formed due to partial carbonization. The dialysate was collected and dried under vacuum using Equitron Roteva, India to obtain solid CDs. In the synthesis, 1 g of neem seed kernel yields 145 mg of brown NeCDs powder. Desired amount of obtained CDs were re-dissolved in water and used for further studies. The obtained CDs have excellent solubility in water and in lower alcoholic solvents.

The synthetic route of carbon dots (NeCDs) adapted in the present study is schematically represented in Scheme 1. The size and morphology of NeCDs were examined using TEM analysis. The resultant TEM image is shown in Fig. 1a. The TEM image portrays well separated spherical nanoparticles and the particle size distribution analyzed over 100 particles (Fig. 1b) shows that they are monodisperse.

The Gaussian fit of the corresponding histogram conveys that the average particle size of NeCDs is ~ 2.2 ± 0.04 nm and the relatively narrower fwhm (~ 1.2 nm) acknowledges the monodispersity of the particles formed. Further, to unravel the extent of crystallinity in NeCDs, powder XRD studies were carried out. The X-ray diffractogram (Fig. 1c) shows a broad peak, centered at 2θ = 24.09º, and the corresponding interlayer spacing of the 002 plane is estimated to be 0.36 nm. When compared to bulk graphite (0.33 nm) the degree of structural order in NeCDs is relatively poor which might be due to the dominance of sp3 carbon domains both in the core and at the surfaces. It was further supplemented by the Raman spectral analysis. The Raman spectrum of NeCDs is shown in Fig. 1d which shows two broad bands with the maximum at ~1359 and 1563 cm−1. The former band with A1g symmetry accounts for the sp3-like disordered graphitic structure and the later band with E2g symmetry represent the vibrations of graphitic sp2 carbons. The ratio of integral area of the two bands (ID/IG) quantifies the degree of disorderliness, and in case of NeCDs it is estimated to be 2.06. Such a large ID/IG value indubitably establishes the amorphous nature of NeCDs and accounts for the relatively larger population of sp3 carbon over sp2 carbon domains. Larger value of ID/IG might have been due to the significantly larger proportion of long chain fatty acids available in the neem seed kernels, the carbon precursor. Thus, the sp3 character of the carbon precursors is predominantly retained in the product formed, which accounts for the observed amorphous nature of the NeCDs.

To gain further insight on the influence of chemical constituents present in the neem seed kernel in governing the chemical functionalities present at the core and surface of NeCDs, the FTIR spectrum of NeCDs was recorded. For a better analysis, the FTIR spectrum of bare neem seed kernel is stacked over NeCDs and it is shown in Fig. 2. The kernel of neem seeds are reported to be composed of triglycerides of unsaturated fatty acids (which includes oleic and linoleic acids), parietal constituents like celluloses, hemicellulose, lignin, some proteins and other isolates [32]. The IR spectrum of neem seed kernel supports the presence of these diverse chemical components. Noticeably, the sharp peak at 1743 cm−1 vindicates the stretching due to carbonyl moiety of the ester group (triglycerides), the peaks at 2852 and 2922 cm−1 (C-H stretching) identifies the presence of long alkyl chains of fatty acids, the broad peak centered at 3281 cm−1 must have been due to O-H stretching of the polyhydroxyl group present in the parietal constituents and the intense peak at 1629 cm−1 evidences the presence of unsaturation (CC stretching).

When compared to neem seed kernel, the FTIR spectrum of NeCDs has shown significant difference. Noticeably, the stretching due to carbonyl moiety is found to be disappeared in case of NeCDs; but relatively larger FWHM of the peak at 1641 cm−1 indicates that the carbonyl stretching in NeCDs might have been shifted to lower wavenumber region and merges with the peak at 1641 cm−1. Such a significant shift towards the lower wavenumber region is anticipated because the synthetic conditions strongly favor the hydrolysis of triglyceric esters of fatty acids (acid catalysed) and it may results in the formation of carboxylic acid functionality which is expected to have absorbance at 1700–1730 cm−1. These results were further supplemented by the XPS analysis which will be discussed later. When compared to its carbon precursor, the absorbance due to O-H stretching in NeCDs is relatively greater than the stretching due to methyl and methylene unit. It may be due to two reasons. Firstly, the hydrolysis of triglyceric ester results in the formation of glycerol, a triol along with the fatty acids. Secondly, the parietal constituents rich with hydroxylic moiety might have passivated the carbon core significantly after carbonization. Thus, the strong absorbance at 3424 cm−1 indicates the effective surface functionalization by the hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties. These interpretations were strongly supplemented by the zeta potential measurements. In case of NeCDs, the zeta potential value was found to be − 50.5 mV, a large negative value (Fig. S1). The negative sign indicates the presence of hydroxyl, phenolic and carboxyl moieties at the surface and such a large deviation from zero indicates that the surface of NeCDs is rich with these negatively charged functional moieties. To further ascertain the chemical composition of NeCDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy studies were carried out and the corresponding survey spectrum is shown in Fig. 3a. It shows only two distinct peaks at the binding energies of 284.5 and 531.0 eV, which corresponds to C 1s and O 1s peaks respectively. It affirms that NeCDs is composed of only carbon and oxygen, other than hydrogen.

Despite the neem seed kernel contains ~10% of protein component (by weight), the doping due to nitrogen (present in amino acids) is not observed in NeCDs either at the core or surfaces. It indicates that the carbonization is predominantly due to esters of fatty acids and parietal constituents present in the neem seed kernel. The high resolution C 1s band and the corresponding peak fitting results were presented in Fig. 3b. It shows that C 1s band is due to the superposition of three distinct peaks identified at 282.7, 284.5 and 286.5 eV, which corresponds to the carbon in the bonding state of C-C/CC, C-O and CO respectively. Similarly, the high resolution O 1s band is found to be composed of peaks due to CO and C-O at 530.7 and 532.3 eV respectively (Fig. 3c). From the XPS analysis, the elemental compositions of NeCDs was calculated: C (80.63%) and O (19.37%). The ratio of integral area of the peaks due to C-O/CO bonding moieties is ~ 2.3, which indubitably establish that the relative population of C-O bond in NeCDs is twice that of CO. All these results conclusively proven that the carbonization was primarily due to the sp3 carbon rich lipid components which are then effectively passivated by the parietal constituents, rich with hydroxyl functionality.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NeCDs was recorded by dispersing the brown NeCDs powder in water and it is shown in Fig. 4a. It shows strong absorption in the UV region and the absorption tail extends in to the visible region up to ~600 nm without any noticeable fine structures. The strong absorption in the UV region is due to π-plasmon absorption of the nano carbon core and the weak absorption tails are reported to be due to multifarious surface states/defects present in CDs. On exposure to 365 nm UV irradiation, the aqueous solution of the NeCDs shows a blue emission (inset of Fig. 4a). On photoexcitation, NeCDs shows strong luminescence in the visible region but the emission maximum (as well as the band) shift towards the red region (from 400 to 550 nm) while shifting the excitation wavelength from 280 to 500 nm (Fig. 4b). This is clearly shown in the corresponding normalized emission spectra in Fig. 4c. Moreover, the emission intensity initially increases with the increase in excitation wavelength and then decreases, such that the optimal emission maximum (~452 nm) was identified at the excitation wavelength of ~380 nm. This excitation dependent PL behavior is typical for most of the CDs and it was reported to be due to i) a function of the size of the nano carbon core (a quantum confinement effect) ii) diverse surface state/defects or iii) the combination of both. To unravel that, excitation spectra of NeCDs were recorded at the different emission maxima and it is presented in Fig. 4d.

The excitation spectra show two well resolved bands, despite there is no such distinct absorption bands was observed in the absorption spectrum; the band with the maximum at ~260 nm accounts for the π-plasmon absorption of the nanocarbon core (referred as C-band) and another band with the maximum at ~366 nm is due to the surface states/defects (referred as S-band). As a function of monitoring emission wavelength, it is both the C- & S-band has shown a significant shift in the excitation band maximum which correlates well with our earlier report on lignite- derived CDs [33]. It indicates that the excitation dependent photophysical behavior of NeCDs is due to the combination of both the function of the size of the nanocarbon core and the diverse functional groups/defects present at the surface of NeCDs. The 2D PL topographical map of NeCDs is given in Fig. 5a which shows two distinct contours at the excitation wavelength of~260 and 360 nm with (almost) a common emission center at ~440 nm i.e. irrespective of the C- (260 nm) or S-band (360 nm) excitation, the optimal PL is observed at ~440 nm. Such a large Stokes shift corresponding to C-band excitation indubitably establishes that even the excitons are generated at the nano carbogenic core it relaxes down to the surface state before recombination. Thus, the PL in NeCDs is predominantly influenced by the surface functional groups/states.

The PL quantum yield (QY) of NeCDs was determined by slope method (Fig. 5b, Table S1) using quinine sulfate as the standard and it is found to be ~12.5% at the excitation wavelength of 366 nm. The PL decay profiles of NeCDs ware recorded using TCSPC by exciting at 375 nm and monitoring the emission at 440 nm which is shown in Fig. 5c. The PL decay curve was fitted as tri-exponential function and the average PL lifetime (τavg) was estimated to be ~2.75 ns (further details are given in Table S2). Relatively moderate PL QY and lifetime value indicates that NeCDs can be a potential material for sensing, energy harvesting and photocatalytic applications.

Our preliminary studies clearly demonstrate that the PL of NeCDs is stable in diverse conditions. However, our detailed characterization analyses unveil that the surface of NeCDs is rich with the negatively charged functional moieties like polyhydroxylic and carboxylic groups, and so it is anticipated that NeCDs may interact with species carrying larger positive charge through either bonding or non-bonding interactions and that may affect its PL. To unravel that PL of NeCDs were recorded in the presence of some of the commonly available metal ions in real aqueous samples (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+) as well as with ions of heavy metals like Ag+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Au3+ at neutral pH. PL intensity of NeCDs is affected only in the presence of aqueous Au3+ ions whereas negligible or no change in emission intensity is observed with all other metal ions even up to the concentration of ~200 μM. Thus the PL intensity of NeCDs is selectively as well as significantly quenched by Au3+ ions and the extent of PL quenching in the presence of different metal ions is presented in Fig. 6a. Considering the importance of detecting the Au3+ ions in aqueous medium (discussed in introduction part), PL response of NeCDs was monitored as the function of increase in concentration of Au3+ ions and it is shown in Fig. 6b. There is a regular decrease in the PL intensity and it is found to be more than 50% even at the concentration of 25 μM of Au3+ ions. The PL response was monitored uniformly after 5 min and by exciting at 380 nm in all cases. The choice of the excitation wavelength (at 380 nm) is based on the fact that the optical density of the analyte (aq. Au3+ ions) at these concentrations is almost nil (above 350 nm) and also the optimal emission maximum for NeCDs is observed at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Similarly, the temporal response for sensing action was restricted to five minutes because the emission intensity quenches well within this time period. The plot of difference in PL intensity (F0-F) vs quencher concentration [Au3+] is presented in Fig. 6c, and it shows a perfect linear relation. Using this plot, the limit of detection (3 X standard deviation/slope) of Au3+ ions was estimated and it is found to be 16 nM, the lowest ever reported than any other PL carbon based probes (compared with existing PL probes in Table S3). Hence, NeCDs can be used as the potential luminescent probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Au3+ ions in aqueous medium.

Highly sensitive and selective quenching of NeCDs PL by aqueous Au3+ ions is alluring and it drives us to explore the rationale behind the sensing action. In the literature, there are only few PL-based probes that detect Au3+ ions in aqueous medium (sensitivity down to ~50 nM) and their mode of sensing action varies significantly from each other. But, limiting our discussion to CDs based PL probes; the sensing/quenching action is possibly due to i) co-ordination-induced aggregation followed by aggregation-caused quenching ii) donor excited photoinduced electron transfer process i.e. d-PET from photoexcited CDs to Au3+ ions iii) ground state redox reaction between Au3+ ions and organic functional groups exist at the surface of CDs at room temperature iv) Energy transfer between photoexcited CDs and Au3+ ions v) change in pH of the medium.

In case of NeCDs, our studies demonstrate that its PL is quite stable for a broader pH range (pH 3–10) and introduction of Au3+ as HAuCl4 in nanomolar concentration will have only a negligible influence in modulating the pH of the medium and hence the observed PL quenching must not have been due to the change in pH of the medium. Similarly, examining the absorption and emission spectrum of AuCl4 ¯ ions and NeCDs unveils that there is only a negligible spectral overlap between the emission band of NeCDs (excited at 380 nm) and the absorption band of AuCl4 ¯ ions. So, the energy transfer between photoexcited CDs and Au3+ ions is also not feasible. The surface of our NeCDs is rich with poly-hydroxyl functional group and hence, there is a definite possibility for the ground state redox reaction between the surface functional group and aqueous Au3+ ions. But as mentioned earlier, the response time given for PL quenching studies is only 5 min, a very short duration which is not sufficient enough for the ground state redox reaction to have an influence on the PL quenching dynamics. This presumption is strongly supplemented by the kinetics of this ground state redox reaction which will be discussed later. Even if it occurs, its impact in observed PL quenching dynamics will be negligible and hence it can also be neglected. But the influence of remaining two PL quenching pathways viz. co-ordination induced aggregation and donor excited PET are equally undeniable and hence need to be evaluated experimentally.

The difference in the nature of quenching interaction between these two quenching processes may aid us to unravel their existence as well as their competence. Co-ordination induced aggregation is purely a ground state interaction which generally results in static quenching of luminescence whereas d-PET is expected to be an excited state process. However the d-PET process may occur via collisional interaction between the excited state of NeCDs (referred as NeCDs*) and Au3+ ions, a collisional quenching mode or the added Au3+ ions might have co-ordinated at the surface of NeCDs and after photoexcitation the transfer of electron may occur from NeCDs* to the self-co-ordinated Au3+ ions, more like an intramolecular PET process. Thus, the observed PL quenching dynamics may be solely due to any one of the above three possibilities or certain combinations of them. After careful experimental analysis, we come up with the conclusion that ‘it is only the combination of d-PET from NeCDs* to the self-co-ordinated Au3+ ions and co-ordination induced aggregation operates in the PL quenching process as a function of quencher concentration (Au3+ ions)’. Experimental validations for the drawn conclusion are discussed below.

Initially, to unravel the existence of these different possibilities, we have performed Stern-Volmer (S-V) analysis for the observed PL quenching dynamics. S-V plot is a plot of ratio of PL intensity in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of quencher (in our case Au3+ ions) at different concentrations ([Q]), as per the following equation. KSV in the equation stands for S-V quenching constant.
F0/F = 1+ KSV [Q]
If it shows a linear relation, the quenching is either purely static or dynamic. But in our case, the S-V plot (F0/F vs [Au3+]) presented in Fig. 7a shows an upward curvature and not a straight line.

It clearly depicts the co-existence of static and dynamic quenching process and the pure static or dynamic process is not responsible for the observed PL quenching dynamics. Further, to ascertain the dynamic portion of the quenching process, PL lifetime measurements were carried out in nanosecond time domain and it is presented in Fig. 7b. To our surprise, the PL decay profile of NeCDs in nanosecond time domain exactly traces one over the other in the absence and presence of quencher (Au3+ ions) at all concentrations. Also, the PL decay profiles of NeCDs fits tri-exponential at all quencher concentrations and there is no change/quenching in its PL lifetime value. These results clearly depict the absence of collisional quenching interaction between the NeCDs and Au3+ ions.

The remaining two PL quenching modes are possible to operate in our system is viz., d-PET from NeCDs* to co-ordinated Au3+ ions and co-ordination induced aggregation. Among which, co-ordination induced aggregation is typically observed in most of the CDs based sensing of metal ions (including Au3+ ions), where the selectivity and sensitivity is offered by the nature and extent of the surface functional groups. In our case, the surface of NeCDs is extensively functionalised by the carboxyl and poly-hydroxyl functional moieties (zeta potential of −50.5 mV), which can effectively facilitate the co-ordination of Au3+ ions followed by the aggregation of CDs. This presumption is experimentally validated by the TEM analysis shown in Fig. 8a–d which clearly demonstrates the agglomeration of NeCDs in the presence of Au3+ ions.

Similarly, to probe the occurrence of d-PET from NeCDs* to co-ordinated Au3+ ions, we have carried out two different sets of experiments. Firstly, the solution mixture of NeCDs and Au3+ ions were exposed to high intense xenon lamp (without focusing it) for a period of 1 h. Within 30 min, the colour of the solution slowly started to change from light brown to pink and it becomes intense after 1 h as shown in the inset of Fig. 9. UV–vis absorption spectrum of the resulting solution was recorded and it shows a strong absorption at ~550 nm, which matches with the surface plasmon absorption of the gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), which is presented in Fig. 9.

Secondly, the solution mixture of NeCDs and Au3+ ions was allowed to withstand at room temperature for 24 h in dark. Within this time period, the light brown solution turns into a sparkling golden yellow suspension and the corresponding photograph is shown in Fig. 10.

TEM and EDX analysis of this suspension indubitably establishes that the sparkling golden yellow suspension is actually the gold nanoparticles, which must have formed due to the ground state redox reaction between the NeCDs and Au3+ ions. These two experiments conclusively proven that the surface functional groups of NeCDs are prone to reduce Au3+ ions under both thermal and photochemical conditions. In fact, in presence of high intense light source the reduction of Au3+ ions completes within an hour.

It indicates that the other quenching process i.e. d-PET from NeCDs* to self-co-ordinated Au3+ ions is equally undeniable. Thus, both these processes are operating in the quenching of NeCDs PL, which may be the reason for such a low LOD value exhibited by NeCDs in detecting the aqueous Au3+ ions. However, if the percentage contributions from both these processes are equally distributed at all quencher concentration, then the S-V plot is again expected to be linear. But instead, we experience an upward curvature (Fig. 7a), which it indicates that the percentage contribution from one of the quenching process increases as a function of quencher concentration. Among these two processes, it is expected that the relative contribution from co-ordination induced aggregation would be very less at lower concentration of Au3+ ions and at high concentration it would play a significant role. It is because the process of aggregation is initiated and facilitated by the metal ion co-ordinated/complexed at the surface of NeCDs, unless otherwise the solution of NeCDs is quite stable for months. So, initially the equilibrium must have established between the NeCDs and Au3+ co-ordinated NeCDs (referred as AuNeCDs), which is then followed by the aggregation as shown in Fig. 11.

Thus, even at relatively lower concentrations, the Au3+ ion complexes at the surface of NeCDs which is on photo-irradiation drives the d-PET process from NeCDs* to the self co-ordinated Au3+ ions. And at higher concentration, both these deactivation processes quenches the NeCDs PL which results in the upward curvature in S-V plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and extraction of urchin-like gold nanoparticles (UL-Au NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: The NeCDs-Au3+ solution, on standing for about 24 h produce yellowish glittering gold (Fig. 10). It indicates that the NeCDs are behaved as a reducing agent towards Au3+ ions. About 29 ± 2 mg of gold (The detailed methodology is described in the supplementary information) was effectively reduced from 0.2 mM Au3+ solution by 1 mg of NeCDs (no increase in reduction was noticed on further increasing the concentration of Au3+ ions). The relative recovery efficiency is about 73.5%. The size, shape and morphology of the reduced gold particles were analyzed by TEM, FE-SEM and AFM. Fig. 12 shows TEM images of as-obtained gold particles, which shows urchin like morphology with the particle size of about 30–105 nm. Fig. 13 shows the FE-SEM image of the UL-Au NPs, the TEM and FE-SEM results confirms that the obtained particles are urchin like shape with rough surface morphology. The EDX spectrum of UL-Au NPs showed highest peak at 2.15 keV it’s corresponding to the UL-Au NPs and the carbon and copper signals also appeared in the EDX spectrum which is due to the carbon coated copper TEM grid employed for the TEM image analysis (Fig. S4). Crystallinity of UL-Au NPs was analyzed by SAED pattern which is shown in Fig. S5. The SAED result confirms the crystalline nature of UL-Au NPs. Surface roughness of the UL-Au NPs was analyzed by AFM technique. From the AFM analysis, the UL-Au NPs showing distinct roughness, skew, kurtosis, and exposed surface area properties. Fig. 14 shows the AFM images of UL-Au NPs. The root mean square (RMS) roughness of the UL-Au NPs is 9.39 and 8.08 which gives the overall surface roughness. The surface analysis shows slightly positive and negative skewness (0.226 and −0.10) and Kurtosis values (−0.162 and 0.69). AFM analysis result confirms that the UL-Au NPs are appears in urchin-like shape, possess asymmetric nature, (flat and sharp peaks with heavy tails). Surfaces with deep narrow valleys results negative skew value whereas those with high narrow peaks results positive skew value in AFM analysis [34].

Kurtosis is a relative measure of whether the collected force data is more peaked (positive) or flat (negative) compared to a normal curve shape. Low (negative or platykurtic) kurtosis means that the data sets have a flat top near the mean; on the other hand, positive (leptokurtic) kurtosis means that the data sets have sharp peaks, rapid declines, and heavy tails [35].

The highly luminescent CDs were synthesized by an environmentally benign, rapid and one-step microwave-assisted method using neem seed kernels, which is abundant, green, inexpensive, sustainable and eco-friendly starting material. The structural and photophysical properties of resulted NeCDs were thoroughly examined. The PL NeCDs exhibit excellent PL stability, including long term storage, high photostability, and good tolerance for pH, ionic strength and photobleaching, which are very important properties for practical applications. The cell viabilities of the NeCDs was also evaluated using A549 cells, and the cell viabilities were found to be greater than 96% over a wide range of concentrations from 10 to 2000 μgmL−1. We also demonstrated that our NeCDs can function as an effective luminescent probe for multicolor cell imaging. Moreover, the NeCDs selectively response to the Au3+ ions and which is also reducing the Au3+ ions to metallic gold. Hence, the NeCDs were successfully utilized as a potential candidate for selective sensing of Au3+ and recovery of gold in aqueous media without adding any other external reducing agents. It is a significant and environmentally benign method to selective sensing and recovery of Au3+ ions as metallic nano gold (UL-Au NPs) in aqueous medium.

P.R. and R.K designed all of the experiments. R.K and T.S carried out all the experiments and data analysis. P.R, R.K, T.S, R.S, V.R, and D.P are validated the results and prepared the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are also thankful to Prof. S. Balakumar, Director, National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (NCNSNT), University of Madras for Raman facilities. Authors thank the DST PURSE programme for the zeta potential analysis facility. The authors gratefully acknowledge Dr. G. Sudhandiran, Dept. of Biochemistry, University of Madras for his assistance on cytotoxicity experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.128523.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
